schools.summary: Gill: the voyage determined by Festus, with the advice of Agrippa and his council, pursuant to Paul's appeal, and founded on the will of God (all concurring) — it was the decree and will of God that Paul should go to Rome (Acts 23:11), made known to him; Paul delivered with other prisoners to Julius, a centurion, for the voyage to Italy — the human decision and the divine will concurring (the providence ordering all to bring Paul to Rome, the sovereign purpose through human means, Proverbs 19:21), the apostle borne toward his appointed witness, Luke the faithful companion ('we').

themes: it was determined that we should sail into Italy
the providence carrying Paul to Rome

schools.summary: Gill: the ship of Adramyttium (a city on the sea shore) in the port of Caesarea, bound for the coasts of Asia; Aristarchus, a Macedonian of Thessalonica (Acts 19:29; 20:4), accompanying Paul — the faithful companion sharing the apostle's perilous voyage, later a fellow-prisoner with him (Colossians 4:10; Philemon 24); the devotion of the true friend (Proverbs 18:24, the love that shares the perils of the persecuted), the comfort of faithful companionship in trial (Paul not left alone, Luke and Aristarchus with him), the providence in the companions.

themes: Aristarchus, a Macedonian of Thessalonica, being with us
the friend who shared Paul's perils

schools.summary: Gill: Sidon a famous maritime city in Phoenicia; Julius courteously treating Paul, giving him liberty at Sidon to visit his friends (the Christians there) and be refreshed — the favour God gave Paul in the eyes of the centurion (the providence raising up kindness for the apostle in custody, as Joseph with the keeper, Genesis 39:21, Daniel with the prince of the eunuchs, Daniel 1:9, Psalm 106:46), the comfort of the fellowship of the saints (refreshed by his brethren), the character that won the favour (the godly life commending the man).

themes: liberty to go unto his friends to refresh himself

schools.summary: Barnes: 'sailing under Cyprus' means they sailed along its coast, keeping near it to break off the violent winds — instead of a direct course in the open sea (exposing them to opposing winds), they kept the island between them and the westerly winds, breaking the force of the wind; the contrary winds the early difficulty of the voyage (foreshadowing the great storm, v.14, the trials beginning, the believer's course beset by tribulation, Acts 14:22); the providence working through the difficulties (the contrary winds not defeating the purpose, Paul still carried toward Rome), the prudent seamanship using skill and means.

Gill: the seas of Cilicia and Pamphylia joined, Myra a city of Lycia; Barnes: the sea off the coast of these provinces; the ship crossing the difficult seas and arriving at Myra (the stage where they would change ships, v.6) — the journey continued through difficulty (against the contrary winds), the providence ordering the course toward Rome (the journey directed by God, the apostle borne onward), the change of ships to come (the providence arranging the means), the perseverance of the voyage (the steady progress through trial, the course held).

themes: sailed over the sea of Cilicia and Pamphylia

schools.summary: Gill: Alexandria the chief city of Egypt, its famous port sending ships for trade, one found at Myra bound for Italy; the centurion finding an Alexandrian ship (a great grain ship of the Egypt-to-Rome corn trade) and putting Paul's company aboard — the providence arranging the means of the journey (the very ship that would meet the storm, v.14, and be wrecked, v.41, yet carry them toward the appointed Rome, all preserved, v.44), the providence ordering even the smallest arrangements (Proverbs 16:9), the large vessel with 276 aboard (v.37) proportioned to the deliverance to come.

themes: a ship of Alexandria sailing into Italy
the ship ordained for the journey

schools.summary: Barnes: they sailed slowly by reason of the prevailing western winds, scarcely able to come over against Cnidus (a city on a promontory in Asia Minor), the wind not suffering them to sail directly, so they sailed under the lee of Crete; Gill: the contrary winds; the slow, laborious sailing against the wind (scarcely reaching Cnidus, forced from the intended route under Crete) — the persistent difficulty (the trials mounting toward the great storm, v.14), the delay consuming the safe season (v.9, bringing them into the dangerous time), the perseverance amid difficulty (the seamanship persisting).

Barnes: they scarcely passed Salmone without being wrecked (almost driven on it), passing round the east end of Crete being unable to sail directly; the Fair Havens, on the south-eastern part of Crete (near Lasea), probably an open road affording good anchorage for a time; the ship reaching the haven with difficulty (the hard-won arrival) — the temporary, precarious shelter (a fair-weather haven, not commodious to winter, v.12), the place of the fateful decision whether to winter or sail on (the crossroads of the voyage), the contrast of the fair name and the coming danger.

themes: came unto a place called The fair havens
the place of the fateful decision

schools.summary: Barnes: much time spent in the difficult sailing — they had hoped to reach Italy before the dangerous season but had been detained; 'the fast' the Day of Atonement (late September/October), after which Mediterranean navigation became perilous; Gill: the long delay; the safe season being past and sailing now dangerous, Paul admonishing the company — the prudent warning of the man of God (whether by his knowledge of navigation, 2 Corinthians 11:25, or divine intimation, perceiving the danger), the apostle's concern for all aboard, the historical precision of the Jewish reckoning (Luke's detail).

Gill: Paul foresaw and foretold the danger by a spirit of prophecy with which he was endued; Barnes: not certain it was direct inspiration — he might have perceived it from his own knowledge of the danger of navigation at the autumnal equinox and from what he saw of the ship; Paul warning of hurt and loss (of cargo, ship, and lives) — the man of God's counsel either prophetic or prudent (the godly man's foresight, the warning wise and true either way); the warning of loss of life not absolute (later, by the angel's message, v.22, he assures no life lost — the danger real, the deliverance of grace).

debates.question: Did Paul warn by prophecy or by prudent judgment?

sides.position: By a spirit of prophecy with which he was endued
Not certainly by inspiration — possibly from his own knowledge of the danger of the season and the ship

themes: this voyage will be with hurt and much damage
not only of the lading... but our lives
the warning of the man of God

schools.summary: Gill: the master (governor/pilot) sat at the helm and directed the ship; the centurion, naturally, believing the master and owner of the ship (the experts in seamanship) rather than Paul's warning — the human counsel preferred to the man of God (the worldly judgment preferring the worldly experts); the error of trusting human expertise against the wisdom of the godly (the event proving Paul right and the experts wrong, 1 Corinthians 1:25, the godly counsel vindicated), the cost of ignoring the warning (leading into the storm and loss), the experts biased by their interest in the cargo (v.12).

themes: the centurion believed the master and owner
the experts preferred to the man of God

schools.summary: Gill: the Fair Havens not commodious to winter in (a good summer haven but an open road exposed to the violent sea in stormy weather, unfit for winter); the majority advising to sail on to Phenice (a better harbour of Crete) to winter — the counsel of the many, the human reasoning preferring the better harbour (the plausible, prudent-seeming counsel that led them out into the storm against Paul's warning); the danger of majority counsel against the godly (the multitude not always right, Exodus 23:2), the providence overruling even the mistaken counsel (through the storm and wreck all brought safe, v.44).

themes: the haven was not commodious to winter in
the more part advised to depart

schools.summary: Barnes: the wind, before a headwind from the west, now veered to the south and blew gently — they supposed they could sail safely along the coast of Crete and set sail; the gentle south wind deceptive (luring them out, but soon giving way to the tempestuous Euroclydon, v.14) — the false security before the storm (the deceptive calm, the favourable appearance that betrayed them, 1 Thessalonians 5:3), the venturing on appearances against Paul's warning (judging by the surface), the providence behind the deceptive calm (bringing them into the storm that would fulfil God's purpose).

themes: when the south wind blew softly
supposing they had obtained their purpose
the false security before the storm

schools.summary: Barnes: the wind beat violently against Crete; 'Euroclydon' (occurring nowhere else) most probably denotes a wind not blowing steadily from any quarter but a hurricane veering about to different quarters (such hurricanes abounding in the Mediterranean); the tempestuous wind arising suddenly against the ship — the violent storm breaking upon them (turning the deceptive calm of v.13 into peril, the swift reversal, the false security shattered), the storm within God's providence (ordered to bring them through the wreck to the appointed end, the stormy wind fulfilling his word, Psalm 148:8).

Barnes: the ship caught suddenly by the tempest, could not resist its violence or be directed, seized by the wind and driven so it became unmanageable; 'we let her drive' = they suffered the ship to be borne along by the wind without attempting to control it; the ship overpowered, driven helpless before the storm — the helplessness of man before the tempest (the seamen's skill availing nothing, the creature's weakness before God's power, Psalm 107:27), the surrender of control, the providence guiding even the helpless drift (toward Malta, v.26, where all would be saved, the ship driven yet by God's hand).

themes: could not bear up into the wind
the helplessness of man before the tempest

schools.summary: Barnes: they ran near a small island (Clauda, south-west of Crete) where the violence of the wind was probably broken; 'we had much work to come by the boat' = they had much difficulty securing the small boat (towed behind the ship), raising it into the ship to prevent its loss; the company, under the brief shelter, struggling with hard labour — the toil and difficulty of the storm-tossed crew (the anxious labour to preserve the means of escape, the struggle for survival), the diligent use of means in the storm, the wise use of the brief respite (the labour in the lull).

themes: much work to come by the boat
the labour to secure the boat

schools.summary: Barnes: having secured the boat, they 'used helps' (ropes, cables, or chains), undergirding the ship — the ancients passing cables from side to side to keep the ship from being destroyed (frapping); fearing the quicksands (the Syrtis off Africa) they struck sail and were driven; the crew using every means of seamanship to preserve the ship — the diligent use of means amid the peril (the effort consistent with dependence on God, Proverbs 21:31, the means and the providence), the prudent fear guiding the measures, the helplessness even amid the means (the ship still 'driven,' the storm prevailing despite the seamanship).

Gill: the condition at sea described to the life by the Psalmist (Psalm 107:26, lifted to the heavens, sinking to the depths); Barnes: they lightened the ship by throwing out part of the cargo; the company, violently tossed by the tempest, lightening the ship by casting out cargo — the increasing peril (the storm mounting, the measures growing more desperate, the extremity deepening), the desperate jettisoning of extremity (the goods sacrificed for survival, Jonah 1:5), the fulfilment of Paul's warning of loss of cargo and ship (v.10, the prophecy confirmed).

Barnes: on the third day they threw over the tackling of the ship — the anchors, sails, cables, baggage, everything not indispensable to its preservation; Clarke: the supernumerary anchors, cables, and baggage; the company casting out the ship's tackling with their own hands on the third day of the storm — the deepening extremity (after the cargo, v.18, now the very equipment, the storm wearing them down), the extremity that strips away all but life (the trial that takes all), the common labour of all hands ('with our own hands,' even Paul and the passengers), the prolonged trial (the storm not abating).

themes: cast out... the tackling of the ship
the sacrifice of the ship's equipment

schools.summary: Gill: when neither sun nor stars appeared in the dark, tempestuous days, they had no means to steer or judge their course (before the compass) and were comfortless; with neither sun nor stars visible for many days and the tempest unrelenting, all hope of being saved taken away — the extremity of human despair (the depth of hopelessness, the giving up of all, the comfortless gloom), the darkest hour at which God's deliverance would come (man's extremity God's opportunity, the night before the dawn, the despair the dark background of the bright promise to come, vv.22-25).

themes: all hope... was then taken away

schools.summary: Gill: the long abstinence from food, not for want of it but through nausea, fear, and the lack of time amid their labours; Matthew Henry: the issue of the distress — they escaped with their lives, and that for Paul's sake; Paul standing forth amid the despair and gently reminding them they should have heeded his warning (v.10) — not to upbraid but to establish his credibility for the good news to follow (v.22, the reminder serving the hope), the man of God vindicated (the event proving him right, the warning fulfilled), the deliverance for the sake of the godly man (the value of the one righteous, v.24).

themes: ye should have hearkened unto me
credibility established for the good news

schools.summary: Barnes: this must have been cheering news to those who had given up all for lost — as Paul had shown wisdom in his former counsel, they might now be more disposed to listen, and he states the reason he believed they would be safe; Paul exhorting the despairing company to be of good cheer and assuring them no life would be lost, though the ship would — the good news brought into the despair (the man of God turning hopelessness into hope by God's promise), the confident certainty of the assurance ('there shall be no loss'), the assurance grounded not in his own judgment but in the angel's message from God (v.23).

themes: I exhort you to be of good cheer
no loss of any man's life... but of the ship

schools.summary: Gill: the angel, a ministering spirit, sent by God and appearing to Paul (likely while he was praying for deliverance); Barnes: God's messages often communicated by angels (Hebrews 1:14); the angel of God standing by Paul (the ground of the assurance of v.22 in the divine message), and Paul's beautiful twofold confession 'whose I am, and whom I serve' — belonging to God ('whose I am,' bought, claimed, possessed, 1 Corinthians 6:19-20) and serving him ('whom I serve,' the devotion of the servant, Romans 1:9, the service flowing from the belonging), the comfort of the relationship (God caring for his own, the ground of the confidence).

themes: there stood by me this night the angel of God
whose I am, and whom I serve

schools.summary: Gill: though Paul knew he should go to Rome, the flesh was weak and he might have fears, which sometimes attend the best of men; Barnes: 'Fear not, Paul' — do not be alarmed at the danger, thou must be brought before Caesar; the angel comforting Paul and assuring the certainty of God's purpose ('thou must be brought before Caesar,' the divine 'must,' Acts 23:11, the appointed end the storm could not defeat), and granting him the lives of all who sailed with him — the whole company spared for the apostle's sake (the godly man a blessing to the ungodly, Genesis 39:5, the many preserved for the one righteous), likely in answer to Paul's prayer.

themes: Fear not, Paul; thou must be brought before Caesar
God hath given thee all them that sail with thee
the company spared for Paul's sake

schools.summary: Robertson: 'for I believe God' is Paul's reason for his own good cheer and for his exhortation — God had spoken, that was enough; Paul repeating the call to good cheer and grounding it in his faith ('I believe God, that it shall be even as it was told me') — the faith that takes God at his word (resting the whole confidence on the bare promise, Romans 4:20, the assurance that the word of God will stand, 2 Timothy 1:12), the faith against all appearances (amid the raging storm, with all human hope gone, v.20), the example of faith for the believer (taking God at his word, confident against appearances).

themes: I believe God, that it shall be even as it was told me
faith taking God at his word

schools.summary: Gill: this circumstance foretold so that, when it came to pass, it might be manifest it was not a fortuitous event of chance but predetermined by God, made known to Paul, and predicted by him (the island Melita/Malta, the fulfilment in Acts 28:1); Paul foretelling they must be cast upon a certain island — the precise prediction marking the deliverance as God's not chance (the providence proved by the fulfilled word, the divine hand confirmed by the event, Psalm 33:11), the divine 'must' (the appointed manner of the deliverance), the salvation through the shipwreck (the deliverance by an unexpected way).

themes: we must be cast upon a certain island

schools.summary: Barnes: the fourteenth night from the commencement of the tempest; 'in Adria' the Adriatic Sea, the name given by the ancients not only to the gulf but to the whole sea between Greece, Italy, and Africa; the sailors at midnight sensing they neared land (by the sound of breakers or the change of the sea); the company driven up and down for fourteen nights — the long endurance of the storm (the prolonged trial, the patience tried), the providence guiding the helpless drift toward the appointed island (Malta, v.26, the ship driven yet by God's hand), the approach to the promised deliverance, the providence in the timing.

themes: the fourteenth night... driven up and down in Adria
the shipmen deemed they drew near to some country

schools.summary: Gill: the sounding line a line with a lead at the end, let down into the water to find the depth, by which they could judge whether they were near land; the sailors sounding and finding the water shoaling from twenty to fifteen fathoms — confirming the nearness of land (the approach tracked by the lead, the shore drawing near), the prudent seamanship (the diligent watching even at midnight in the storm, the means employed), the nearness of land both hope and peril (the danger of running aground on rocks, v.29, the shore a danger as well as a deliverance).

themes: sounded, and found it twenty fathoms

schools.summary: Gill: they feared falling upon rocks or shelves as the water shallowed so fast; Barnes: they cast four anchors to make the ship secure against the violence of the storm, and wished for the day to discern their situation and danger; the sailors, fearing the rocks, casting four anchors from the stern and longing for daylight — the prudent measures and the anxious waiting (the night of suspense, the longing for the light to reveal their hope, Psalm 130:6), the prudent measures alongside the promise (though Paul had promised safety, vv.22-24, the means and the assurance together, the diligence not slackened), the picture of hope deferred (the soul longing for the dawn after the night of trial).

themes: fearing... rocks, they cast four anchors
the anxious waiting for the dawn

schools.summary: Gill: the shipmen about to flee to save their lives, concluding the ship in the utmost danger; Barnes: they pretended ('under colour') it was necessary to get into the boat to carry the anchors ahead, but with a real intention to make for the shore, leaving the prisoners; the sailors' deceitful attempt to flee in the boat and abandon the ship — the selfish, deceitful attempt to save themselves at the others' peril (the treachery, the desertion of the helpless, the self-love of the natural man in danger), the flight that would have doomed the rest (the ship lost without their skill), the contrast with Paul's care for all (vv.24, 31).

themes: the shipmen were about to flee
under colour... cast anchors out of the foreship

schools.summary: Gill: Paul directed his speech to the centurion and soldiers (not the master and owner, who may have been in the scheme), who depended on his assurance; Paul declaring that unless the sailors (with their skill) stayed in the ship, the rest could not be saved — the harmony of the certain promise and the necessary means (though God had promised all would be saved, v.24, the means must be used, the providence working through means, the end and the means appointed together), the answer to fatalism (the certain promise not breeding idleness but requiring the means), the watchfulness of Paul perceiving and foiling the sailors' design.

debates.question: Does God's certain promise of salvation exclude the use of means?

sides.position: No — the means (the sailors staying) are as appointed as the end; the providence works through means, the certain promise requiring not idleness but the use of means

themes: except these abide in the ship, ye cannot be saved
the certain promise and the necessary means joined

schools.summary: Barnes: the mariners had not yet got into the boat; by cutting the ropes and letting the boat fall away, the soldiers removed all possibility of the sailors' fleeing, compelling them to remain and use their skill for the common safety; the soldiers, trusting Paul's word (v.31), cutting the boat loose — the decisive action securing the means of deliverance (the sailors bound to the ship, the treachery of v.30 foiled), the prompt obedience to the man of God's word (the soldiers who once preferred the experts to Paul, v.11, now trusting and acting, the change wrought by the fulfilled warning), the providence inclining them to heed.

themes: the soldiers cut off the ropes of the boat
the decisive action securing the means

schools.summary: Barnes: at daybreak, before they had light to act, Paul urged them to take food; 'having taken nothing' cannot mean they had eaten nothing at all, but that they had had no regular meal, being too anxious and busy; Paul urging the whole company, worn by fourteen days of fasting and toil, to take food — the apostle's practical, fatherly care for their bodily need (the leader providing for the strength of all before the final effort), the wisdom that tends the body as well as the soul (1 Corinthians 6:13), the leadership of Paul (the prisoner become the head of the company by grace), the prudence of strengthening before the effort.

themes: Paul besought them all to take meat

schools.summary: Barnes: 'not an hair fall from the head' a proverbial expression denoting they should be preserved entirely safe, none lost, experiencing not the least harm (1 Samuel 14:45; 1 Kings 1:52; Matthew 10:30); Paul urging them to eat for their health and strength and renewing the assurance that not a hair of any would perish — the practical counsel (food for the body's good) joined to the confident promise (the complete, perfect preservation, every detail in God's care, Matthew 10:30), the confidence renewed (vv.22, 24), the harmony of the means of strength and the assurance of safety (the diligence and the faith together).

themes: take some meat: for this is for your health
not an hair fall from the head of any of you

schools.summary: Gill: this not the Lord's supper (as some suggested) but common bread such as seafaring men eat, the apostle giving thanks before eating; Paul taking bread, giving thanks to God before all, breaking it, and beginning to eat — the example of thanksgiving and calm faith (the apostle openly owning God even amid the storm, the grace before meat, 1 Thessalonians 5:18), the leadership by example (leading the company not only by word but by his own calm faith, the example that encouraged), the calm faith displayed (the trust that could give thanks in the tempest, the peace of the believing heart).

themes: he took bread, and gave thanks to God

schools.summary: Ellicott: the striking contrast to the despair of v.20 — the hearty cheerfulness of the apostle had communicated itself to his companions, as by a kind of electric sympathy, they looking to him as their friend and leader and having spirits to eat once more; the whole company, encouraged by Paul's words and example, taking heart and eating — the contagion of courage from the man of faith (the faith of one lifting the hearts of many, the leader's confidence communicated), the influence of the believer (the calm faith of one godly man lifting all, Job 4:3), the despair turned to cheer (the hopelessness of v.20 transformed by the apostle's confidence).

themes: then were they all of good cheer

schools.summary: Ellicott: the number given here either as a fact omitted before, or because then for the first time, when they were all gathered at their meal, the writer had taken pains to count them (a man does not commonly count the passengers until such an occasion); the company numbering 276 souls — the whole company God had given to Paul (v.24, all spared for his sake), the many preserved for the one man of God (the value of the godly to those around them, the blessing through the one), Luke's precise eyewitness record (calling them 'souls,' the precious lives), the counting at the meal Paul provided (vv.35-36).

themes: two hundred threescore and sixteen souls
the many preserved for the one

schools.summary: Barnes: having no hope of saving the cargo and no further use for it, they threw the wheat into the sea, hoping the ship would draw less water and come nearer the shore; the company, refreshed by the meal (vv.35-36), casting out the remaining wheat to lighten the ship for the run to shore — the final preparation for the deliverance (the means employed for the landing, the effort joined to the promise), the sacrifice of the last of the cargo (the goods given up for survival), the strength restored for the labour (the meal serving the effort, the wisdom of Paul's counsel, v.34, shown).

themes: they lightened the ship, and cast out the wheat
the means employed for the landing

schools.summary: Barnes: they had been driven by the tempest without observation and were strangers to the coast; they discovered a bay (creek) with a level, accessible shore, into which they purposed to run the ship; the company at daybreak not knowing the land but spying a bay with a beach where they hoped to run the ship aground — the dawn revealing the hope of escape (the day long longed for, v.29, come, joy in the morning, Psalm 30:5), the providence bringing them to a place fit for landing (the helpless drift directed to the right place, the unknown land yet the appointed place), the human purpose joined to the providence (the seamanship serving the deliverance).

themes: when it was day, they knew not the land
a certain creek with a shore
the providence bringing them to a fit place

schools.summary: Barnes: they cut or slipped the four anchors (leaving them in the sea), loosed the rudder-bands (which had been tied while at anchor), hoisted the foresail to the wind, and made for the shore; the company taking up the anchors, loosing the rudder, hoisting the sail, and steering for the shore — the final, concerted effort for the landing (the united work, the final push), the diligent use of every means in the run for deliverance (the seamanship employed to the last), the harmony of the means and the promise once more (though God had promised deliverance, v.24, the company diligently using every means, the effort and the faith together).

themes: loosed the rudder bands, and hoised up the mainsail

schools.summary: Barnes: 'a place where two seas met' a place where the waves dashed from different directions; being carried by wind and waves, the ship ran aground, the bow stuck fast, and the stern was broken by the violence of the waves; the ship running aground and breaking up — the wreck fulfilling Paul's word (v.22, 'no loss of any man's life, but of the ship,' the prophecy confirmed, the loss of the ship foreseen), the loss of the ship yet the saving of the souls (the vessel destroyed but all preserved, v.44, the deliverance through the wreck), the providence in the wreck (the destruction the means of the deliverance, cast upon the shore).

themes: the hinder part was broken with the violence of the waves
the ship lost but the souls saved

schools.summary: Barnes: the soldiers' motive probably the strict Roman military discipline — if the prisoners escaped, the soldiers would be held responsible and charged with negligence (punished, as Acts 12:19), so they proposed to kill the prisoners; the soldiers counselling to kill the prisoners (including Paul) lest any escape — the cruel self-interest that would have slain the innocent (the man of God to whom they owed their lives, the ingratitude, the cruelty of self-preservation), the danger to Paul at the very moment of deliverance (the peril at the brink of safety), the providence about to intervene (the centurion sparing Paul, v.43).

themes: the soldiers' counsel was to kill the prisoners
lest any should swim out, and escape

schools.summary: Gill: the centurion willing to save Paul, not only as a Roman citizen and an extraordinary person but chiefly moved by a superior influence, that the will of God might be fulfilled (that Paul go to Rome); Barnes: the centurion's kindness shown from the first (v.3) and confirmed by Paul's conduct and wisdom; the centurion forbidding the killing and ordering the swimmers to make for land — the providence using the centurion's regard for Paul to save all the prisoners (all spared for Paul's sake, the man of God the means of the whole company's deliverance, the many saved for the one, as v.24), the value of the godly to those around them.

themes: the centurion, willing to save Paul, kept them from their purpose
they which could swim should cast themselves first

schools.summary: Barnes: the rest (those who could not swim) escaped on boards and broken pieces of the ship; 'they escaped all safe to land,' according to the promise to Paul (v.22), by the special Providence of God — a remarkable instance of divine interposition to save so many through such dangers, showing God can defend in any peril and accomplish all his purposes; the complete fulfilment of the promise (not one life lost, the word of God proved true, the faith of Paul vindicated, v.25), the providence saving all (over the whole voyage, all things working together for good, Romans 8:28, the purpose accomplished, Paul toward Rome).

themes: some on boards... some on broken pieces
they all escaped safe to land
the promise fulfilled, not one life lost
